text:
Electrochemical synthesis of ammonia via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has emerged as a compelling, sustainable alternative to the traditional Haber-Bosch process. While much of the recent literature focuses on removing low concentrations of nitrate from wastewater, high-concentration nitrate streams—common in nuclear and fertilizer industries—remain largely unexplored. Achieving high-rate production in these concentrated electrolytes (≥1 M) is typically sabotaged by sluggish hydrogen transfer kinetics. 

In this work, we demonstrate that a cocatalytic system featuring Ru clusters on a Cu₂O catalyst overcomes this bottleneck. Using a 16 cm² flow electrolyzer, we achieved a total current of 10.0 A in a 1 M nitrate electrolyte with 100% Faradaic efficiency toward ammonia. We utilized deuterium labeling and *operando* Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to investigate the underlying mechanism, specifically probing the hydrogen transfer rates and intermediate species on the Ru/Cu₂O surface. Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations further reveal that adsorbed hydroxide on the ruthenium nanoparticles densifies the hydrogen-bonded water network near the Cu₂O surface. this structural change accelerates the hydrogen transfer rate, effectively pairing the kinetics of water splitting with nitrate reduction. Our findings underscore the necessity of engineering synergistic cocatalyst interactions to resolve kinetic hurdles in high-performance electrosynthesis.
